metadata.source_title: Sustainability is becoming a pressing necessity for software companies

metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: As our society becomes ever more digital, the influence of software companies grows. They shape how we keep in touch with friends, what information we consume and how we travel. Because of that prominent role, software companies are facing closer scrutiny from both citizens and government.
metadata.parent_text: For example, a recent [court case](https://fd.nl/ondernemen/1351533/baanbrekende-zaak-over-algoritmes-uber-waarschuwing-voor-andere-bedrijven) against taxi company Uber will clarify what ‘transparency’ means in the context of algorithmic decision-making. Software companies would therefore be wise to follow new cases on data processing closely. Today, using data goes hand in hand with deploying Artificial Intelligence (AI), a set of technologies that mimic (elements of) human intelligence and use large volumes of data to do so. AI can unfortunately lead to unintended discrimination, for example when used in recruitment software. The European Commission therefore doubts whether current GDPR legislation suffices for effective enforcement and is exploring specific regulation on artificial intelligence. In 2021, the Commission will present a proposal, including specific attention to preventing discrimination.

The European Commission therefore doubts whether current GDPR legislation suffices for effective enforcement and is exploring specific regulation on artificial intelligence. In 2021, the Commission will present a proposal, including specific attention to preventing discrimination.

text: Until then, software companies can already assess themselves against the [Assessment List for Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence](https://futurium.ec.europa.eu/en/european-ai-alliance/pages/altai-assessment-list-trustworthy-artificial-intelligence). Although the legal framework on data protection and AI ethics is still developing, software companies can already prepare for the relevant themes.

Although this imbalance is partly due to the still modest number of women choosing a technical education, software companies also bear responsibility. A non-inclusive corporate culture is one of the main [reasons](https://www.accenture.com/_acnmedia/PDF-134/Accenture-A4-GWC-Report-Final1.pdf#zoom=50) why women leave tech companies. This masculine culture—also known as ‘brogrammer culture’—also makes it harder to hire women at all, because it seeps into recruitment and selection. For example, women may feel less addressed by a vacancy, or ultimately lose out to a male candidate.

A non-inclusive corporate culture is one of the main [reasons](https://www.accenture.com/_acnmedia/PDF-134/Accenture-A4-GWC-Report-Final1.pdf#zoom=50) why women leave tech companies. This masculine culture—also known as ‘brogrammer culture’—also makes it harder to hire women at all, because it seeps into recruitment and selection. For example, women may feel less addressed by a vacancy, or ultimately lose out to a male candidate.

metadata.parent_text: Energy consumption. It is only a matter of time before software companies are drawn into the current debate about data centres and their energy use. For now, this mainly concerns the tech giants who are placing their ‘hyperscalers’ in the Dutch polder and the municipalities that grant permits. Software companies are also key players in this issue. Developers of digital services often buy the cloud services that run in these data centres. For instance, Netflix largely relies on Amazon’s cloud, and Zoom uses cloud services from Amazon, Microsoft and Oracle. Software companies can influence data centres’ energy consumption—indirectly—by choosing a cloud provider based on the extent to which the data centre uses renewable energy and on the facility’s energy efficiency. Developers can also build their software to require less memory, data storage or data traffic. That gives them a direct influence on data centres’ energy use. For example, cleaning up and restructuring code (‘software refactoring’) can make a programme substantially more energy-efficient. Unfortunately, energy-efficient software is still largely uncharted territory; programmers’ knowledge in this area is [limited](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/308789636_What_do_programmers_know_about_the_energy_consumption_of_software), and measuring the energy consumption of different software components is complex.

metadata.parent_text: So it takes a clear intent during development to limit a product’s footprint. This applies in particular to software that uses artificial intelligence. Training the underlying models uses [a lot of energy](https://medium.com/pcmag-access/ai-could-save-the-world-if-it-doesnt-ruin-the-environment-first-c8cd7e0de76c), and energy use rises exponentially for the last few percentage points of extra ‘accuracy’. Developers of facial recognition software could ask themselves whether those few extra correctly recognised faces outweigh the extra energy used to improve the model. They face persistent labour market shortages, and both citizens and government are setting ever more demands on digital solutions. For these companies, themes such as data protection and AI ethics, diversity and energy efficiency are not empty phrases or attempts at ‘greenwashing’.
